chaptername: Preface	Gateway to Infinite Dharma Treasure
chapterbody: n this book, I want to tell everyone that I am the male protagonist in the book, and I am a treasure trove of dharma.
chapterbody 2: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 3: Why am I a treasure trove of dharma? The reasons are as follows:
chapterbody 5: Firstly:
chapterbody 6: I am gifted.
chapterbody 7: After my divine eye opened, I observed that I am actually the Padmakumara. I also journeyed to the Maha Twin Lotus Ponds, which is the Pure Land of the Padmakumara.
chapterbody 8: Sentient beings are in delusion; the enlightened being wants to wake up the ones in delusion.
chapterbody 9: I composed this verse:
chapterbody 11: The original dharma body transcends the heavens.
chapterbody 12: An ancient pagoda and Buddhist relics however appeared;
chapterbody 13: Only for the sake of a great cause in the world of human beings, Transformed into a monk and remained with sentient beings.
chapterbody 15: Who is Padmakumara? He is the Lotus-hand Bodhisattva, Xiangguang Youth, Baoyi Youth, Baoshang Youth, White Lotus King, White Maha Padmakumara...
chapterbody 16: He is the Lotus Light Unhindered Buddha.
chapterbody 17: Because of the above innate gift, I am a treasure trove of dharma.
chapterbody 19: Secondly:
chapterbody 21: I have attained spiritual union.
chapterbody 22: I have an affinity with cultivation, have practiced the Path of Accumulation, the Path of Preparation, the Path of Seeing, the True Path, and have practiced the Ultimate Path.
chapterbody 23: In Vajrayana Buddhism, I have practiced the Generation Stage perfectly.
chapterbody 24: In Vajrayana Buddhism, I have practiced the Completion Stage perfectly.
chapterbody 25: I have attained spiritual union in the Preliminary Practices, Guru Yoga, Principal Deity Yoga, Heruka Practices, Highest Yoga Tantra, and the Great Perfection.
chapterbody 27: In this life, I only engage myself in cultivation, writing, and disseminating the Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 28: I have attained spiritual union with Golden Mother of the Jade Pond, Amitabha Buddha, Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva and many more.
chapterbody 29: Because I am spiritually awakened, I am a treasure trove of dharma.
chapterbody 31: Thirdly:
chapterbody 33: I have awakened the mind.
chapterbody 35: The awakened mind is being enlightened. I understand that all the phenomenal realms should be viewed as an illusion, a bubble, a shadow, dew, or lightning. I understand the past mind, the present mind, and the future mind are all unobtainable. There is absolutely nothing to be attained.
chapterbody 37: I understand emptiness.
chapterbody 38: I understand nirvana.
chapterbody 39: I understand that “I alone am the honored one in the heavens and on earth.”
chapterbody 40: After I awakened the mind, I realized the ultimate oneness, which is not two in opposition; it is absolute, not relative.
chapterbody 41: After awakening the mind, all my thoughts are calm, and everything is extinguished. Awakening is the totally empty and quiescent state which is constant and without discrimination.
chapterbody 43: The great wisdom is known by few people, so I am a treasure trove of dharma.
chapterbody 45: Fourthly:
chapterbody 46: I see the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 47: Mind awakening is theoretical, a realization only in principle, whereas seeing the Buddha-nature is practical, realizing it by actual practices.
chapterbody 48: The Buddhist teachings are vast, and there are various schools. Each school and sect have their own respective practices. In Vajrayana Buddhism, the Great Perfection, Mahamudra, Yamantaka, and the Great Perfection Profound Wisdom are four great dharmas that I have practiced for forty years.
chapterbody 49: I have witnessed the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 50: Witnessing the Buddha-nature is rare in the world, so I am a treasure trove of dharma.
chapterbody 52: Finally, what I want to tell everyone is:
chapterbody 54: If you read the book, you will also have a life without regrets.
chapterbody 56: Through my wisdom eye, the world, life, universe, cause and effect, reincarnation, nirvana, and everything are all an enormous illusion.
chapterbody 57: What I found is the ultimate of the Buddhadharma. Once reaching the ultimate, one will realize that even the Buddhadharma is not the Buddhadharma anymore. I have endless supernatural powers and subtle perceptions. Because of my wisdom, I have no regrets in my life!
chapterbody 58: All will praise this book!

chapterbody: would like to begin by telling a joke:
chapterbody 2: After turning in for the night, Mrs. Zhu chattered endlessly about some trivial household matters. Her husband however did not utter a word.
chapterbody 3: After a while of jabbering, Mrs. Zhu stared at her husband and stated, “I have been talking all by myself. It is not fair. Now it’s your turn to say something, and I’ll listen.”
chapterbody 4: Mr. Zhu nodded and replied, “Good! I will speak now. It is late, so let’s sleep!”
chapterbody 5: Hahaha!
chapterbody 7: I say:
chapterbody 9: What Mr. Zhu has said is the essence and key. When it is time for bed, sleep!
chapterbody 10: This book does not contain long-winded theories. I will not use deep Buddhist terminology, nor will I lecture or preach. I just want to explain what I have achieved during my own practice, using the simplest language.
chapterbody 12: Remove branches and leaves.
chapterbody 13: Directly to the point.
chapterbody 14: Not talking much.
chapterbody 15: Only the essence.
chapterbody 17: For example:
chapterbody 18: A disciple asked, “Grand Master Lu came to the human world in this lifetime, what is the purpose?”
chapterbody 19: I replied, “To save sentient beings!”
chapterbody 21: The disciple asked, “What if someone does not believe you?”
chapterbody 22: I replied, “Continue to proclaim with a loud voice!”
chapterbody 24: The disciple asked, “What if someone already believes you?”
chapterbody 25: I replied, “Persist to proclaim with a loud voice!”
chapterbody 27: The disciple asked, “Why do you always proclaim with a loud voice?”
chapterbody 28: I replied, “Always continue to proclaim with a loud voice!”
chapterbody 30: The disciple asked, “Anything else?”
chapterbody 31: I replied, “No!”
chapterbody 33: Hahaha! I came to this world just to “announce with a loud voice,” nothing else. That is the key.
chapterbody 35: The disciple asked, “Why does Grand Master Lu want to announce with a loud voice? Are you crazy?”
chapterbody 36: I replied, “I am an enlightened being who has awakened the mind and seen the Buddha-nature. Sentient beings are in delusion; the enlightened being wants to wake up the ones in delusion.”
chapterbody 38: The disciple asked, “Are you crazy?”
chapterbody 39: I replied, “Yes, in the eyes of sentient beings, I’m a crazy dharma king, a crazy practitioner.”
chapterbody 41: The disciple asked, “You claim you are enlightened, who can prove this?”
chapterbody 42: I replied, “No one can prove that I am enlightened, except the emptiness. I realized the emptiness; the emptiness has proven my realization. This is how “The dharma light will be turned on followed by the self-light being on.”
chapterbody 44: I would like to tell everyone, my key:
chapterbody 45: “Emptiness.”
chapterbody 46: It is simple.
chapterbody 47: It is clear.
chapterbody 48: It is pure.
chapterbody 49: It goes beyond name and form.
chapterbody 51: “Emptiness” is absolute, and not two in opposition.
chapterbody 52: “If you say existence?”
chapterbody 53: “Existence is also within the emptiness.”
chapterbody 56: I’ll reply,
chapterbody 57: Emptiness and existence are both extinguished!
chapterbody 65: People with the wisdom eye see the instantaneous changes and understand that all things are illusory and unreal.

chapterbody: et me start with a joke.
chapterbody 2: A person entered a noodle restaurant, and consumed a bowl of beef noodles, but found no beef with the noodles.
chapterbody 3: He asked the manager, “Manager! Manager! There is no beef in my beef noodles!”
chapterbody 4: The manager sarcastically replied, “When you eat a Sun Cake, does the Sun Cake have a sun in it?”
chapterbody 5: The guest was speechless.
chapterbody 6: Hahaha!
chapterbody 7: This joke has a profound meaning. “The Ultimate Key Points” appear within the joke.
chapterbody 8: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 9: I have often remarked:
chapterbody 10: The Sun Cake has no sun within.
chapterbody 11: The Moon Cake has no moon within.
chapterbody 12: The Wife Cake has no wife within.
chapterbody 13: (Recently, Taiwan has had a controversy over cooking oils. It has been discovered in Taiwan that olive oil has no olives, sesame oil has no sesames, peanut oil has no peanuts, and pepper oil has no pepper, etc.)
chapterbody 15: The key points are:
chapterbody 16: “Non-existence, non-existence, non-existence,” that is “nothing, nothing, nothing,” and is also “emptiness, emptiness, emptiness.”
chapterbody 18: Thus, I often use the moon as an example. What is on the moon? There are no human beings, cars or houses on the moon. There is nothing on the moon. The moon has no privileges, no money, and no lust.
chapterbody 20: I ask:
chapterbody 21: On the moon, what is good? What is evil? What is right? What is wrong? What is the Buddha? What is Mara? What is happiness? What is anger? What is righteousness? What is wickedness? Etc.
chapterbody 23: I tell you, “Everything is a sea of clarity and serenity.”
chapterbody 24: This is “no mind and no form.”
chapterbody 26: The key points are:
chapterbody 27: Everyone believes that people and everything on the Earth truly exist because they seem so real.
chapterbody 28: But, we can think in such a way:
chapterbody 29: “People will die.”
chapterbody 30: “Things will end.”
chapterbody 31: “Objects will decay.”
chapterbody 33: All tangible things on Earth cannot avoid the cycle of “formation, existence, decay, and disappearance.” People with the wisdom eye see the instantaneous changes and understand that all things are illusory and unreal. If a practitioner can think in this manner, sooner or later they will be enlightened by the ultimate key point.
chapterbody 35: Remembering and practicing that thought is of foremost importance.
chapterbody 36: Remembering and practicing the key point, one can avoid distorted and deluded perceptions, far from greed, anger, ignorance, and afflictions.
chapterbody 37: Thus:
chapterbody 38: External phenomena become void and tranquil.
chapterbody 39: Emptying the mind within.
chapterbody 40: Be quiescent both internally and externally
chapterbody 41: How could the deluded mind arise?
chapterbody 43: Think about “the moon in the water,” “the flower in the mirror,” and yourself in the Saha world. What do you really obtain? By thinking this way, one will be thoroughly enlightened!
chapterbody 45: There is a poem in Dream of the Red Chamber. I shortened it as follows:
chapterbody 47: Mean hovels and abandoned halls,
chapterbody 48: Where courtiers once paid daily calls.
chapterbody 49: Bleak haunts where weeds and willows scarcely thrive,
chapterbody 50: Were once with mirth and revelry alive.
chapterbody 52: Would you of perfumed elegance recite?
chapterbody 53: With fresh rouge and fragrant foundation,
chapterbody 55: Now...in a trice a tramp by all reviled.
chapterbody 56: Even as you speak, the raven locks turn white.
chapterbody 57: The temple hair turns white.
chapterbody 58: Coffers with gold and silver filled,
chapterbody 59: Not knowing that he too goes home - to die!
chapterbody 60: As each singer leaves, another takes the stage.
chapterbody 65: One at some other’s short life gives a sigh.
chapterbody 66: In such commotion does the world’s theatre rage!

chaptername: 003 Stage of Insight and Cultivation of the Mind
chapterbody: et me tell you a joke first!
chapterbody 3: A thin and weak lady by the name of Mrs. Hsu was re-elected President of the Women’s Association. When she came on stage to give a speech, she humorously said, “I have been elected President of the association consecutively a few times. When everyone sees me, they shout President, President [same pronunciation in Chinese as being able to grow taller]. However, in fact, I’m still short. I am not growing any taller.”
chapterbody 5: Hahaha!
chapterbody 6: I can honestly tell you, if you are a person with an enlightened mind, you must surely know:
chapterbody 7: Neither increasing nor decreasing.
chapterbody 8: Neither impurity nor purity.
chapterbody 9: Neither coming nor going.
chapterbody 10: Neither taking nor giving.
chapterbody 11: The Buddha-nature is primordial and constant. It does not grow!
chapterbody 12: We cultivators must first have this stage of insight, and this insight is the key point.
chapterbody 14: I will tell a second joke!
chapterbody 15: A wife said, “There was a man who was sentenced to one year in prison, because he married two wives.”
chapterbody 16: The husband replied, “He’s so lucky! I just married one wife, but was sentenced to a lifetime of imprisonment.”
chapterbody 18: Hahaha!
chapterbody 20: I tell you, this is a joke about being “single-minded.”
chapterbody 21: The most important thing as a cultivator is being “single-minded.”
chapterbody 22: First become aware of the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 23: Then we must keep in mind and constantly practice, also known as maintaining one’s practice after awakening. This is “single-minded,” and being “single-minded” is very important.
chapterbody 25: Cognition of the Buddha-nature, keeping the cognition in mind, and practicing single-mindedly and constantly are the skills and key for mind cultivation.
chapterbody 26: Cognition is the principle.
chapterbody 27: Keeping in mind and practicing continuously is to realize the principle.
chapterbody 28: Just understanding the Buddha-nature is of no use if the understanding is only in principle. You must always keep in mind, never forget, maintain and practice the principle. This is the effort of actually realizing the cognition.
chapterbody 30: Let me give you an example:
chapterbody 31: A lamp is only external in appearance.
chapterbody 32: It needs to be connected to an electrical source in order to emit light.
chapterbody 33: The electrical connection must be single-minded. (The electricity must remain on.)
chapterbody 34: After having gained this stage of insight, you must go to cultivate the mind. Having gained this stage of insight can only be considered teaching, but does not benefit cultivating the mind at all. Cultivating the mind is practice.
chapterbody 36: One Zen master said:
chapterbody 37: Enlightenment is like riding a bull.
chapterbody 38: Cultivation of the mind is like controlling the bull and not trampling on the garden.
chapterbody 39: Therefore, developing bodhicitta and not hurting people are very important key points.
chapterbody 40: Do not hurt people!
chapterbody 41: But help people instead!
chapterbody 42: This is the key point of being a bodhisattva.
chapterbody 44: I sincerely tell all of you:
chapterbody 45: At the beginning of studying Buddhism
chapterbody 46: The goal is to attain the awareness of the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 47: At the end of studying Buddhism
chapterbody 48: The goal is to manifest the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 49: This is the stage of insight and cultivating the mind.
chapterbody 51: Keeping in mind and practicing continuously are the unsurpassed key points.
chapterbody 52: Do you want to be free?
chapterbody 53: Do you want to be unrestrained?
chapterbody 54: Then you must go to find a master who is truly enlightened and can teach one the utmost important key points.
chapterbody 55: He is right in front of you.
chapterbody 56: Do you want to be happy?
chapterbody 58: Who is the most worthy enlightened master?
chapterbody 60: Where there is life, there must be death. This is the immutable, universal truth.

chapterbody: ne day, we went to watch a movie, it was “The Hobbit.”
chapterbody 3: Some of the characters in the movie included:
chapterbody 4: Hobbits,
chapterbody 5: Orcs,
chapterbody 6: Elf people, and
chapterbody 7: An evil dragon.
chapterbody 9: The movie began with a king at the foot of a mountain who tried to regain their ancestral power, gold, silver, and jewelry.
chapterbody 11: This movie has no beginning or end. In the movie, if you do not connect the story with “The Lord of the Rings,” then essentially there is no beginning and no ending.
chapterbody 13: The fighting in the movie was exciting.
chapterbody 14: Our minds were captivated by the thrilling fight scenes.
chapterbody 15: Only at the conclusion of the movie, could we breathe a sigh of relief. Whew! Moreover, I was I and the movie was the movie.
chapterbody 17: The conclusion:
chapterbody 18: “We just watched a movie!”
chapterbody 20: Therefore, “Life is merely a drama playing out.” This is the key point I want to impart to everyone.
chapterbody 21: A practitioner should observe the drama as a bystander. No matter what happens in the drama, one should constantly remain a bystander.
chapterbody 22: When one reviews one’s own life from birth to death, is it not a drama?
chapterbody 23: One must awaken from this drama. By always observing as a bystander, one will attain realization.
chapterbody 25: One laughs out loud and realizes this is how it is. This is how it is. Life is like watching and acting in a movie.
chapterbody 26: When the drama ends, one should be enlightened! Isn’t your body and mind free now?
chapterbody 27: We best be “bystanders.” One acting in this manner is a sensible person.
chapterbody 28: Even if you are the main character, you should understand that life is just a drama. As a result, your body and mind are liberated.
chapterbody 30: Here is a joke:
chapterbody 31: Mr. and Mrs. Lu went shopping. After coming back, Mrs. Lu happily said, “Earlier on the road, lots of men looked at me!”
chapterbody 32: Mr. Lu replied coldly, “Let them look! Otherwise, as soon as you come home, no one at all will look at you!”
chapterbody 34: Hahaha!
chapterbody 35: From this joke, what can you sense?
chapterbody 36: When you are alive, you act and watch.
chapterbody 37: Upon your death, the drama ends.
chapterbody 39: No one can avoid death. Fame, status, power and beauty all change ownership at the moment of death.
chapterbody 40: Where there is life, there must be death. This is the immutable, universal truth.
chapterbody 41: You’re an actor.
chapterbody 42: You are also a bystander.
chapterbody 44: Hey brother!
chapterbody 45: Take it easy! Why so nervous?
chapterbody 46: In this world, what’s the big deal?
chapterbody 47: It’s just a drama!
chapterbody 49: The key point of my article is “life is a drama.” Only in this way, can one be in an “unoccupied” mood, be of “no-thought” attitude, and enter the “single-minded” Samadhi (meditation).
chapterbody 51: If you are still:
chapterbody 52: Ignorant.
chapterbody 53: Scatterbrained.
chapterbody 54: Struggling.
chapterbody 56: Afflicted.
chapterbody 57: Stubborn.
chapterbody 58: Ugh! What else can I say?
chapterbody 66: The three sentences are drawn from the truths realized by Shakyamuni Buddha, which are impermanence, no self, nirvana, and quiescence.

chapterbody: ecently I often walk by Shizheng Road in Taichung, Taiwan. While walking by, I noticed a large banner that read “Peace of mind - Peace” hung high on a building under construction.
chapterbody 3: The banner reminded me of the six characters in the airport’s VIP lounge written by the Venerable Master Sheng Yen:
chapterbody 4: “Peace of Mind Is Peace.”[In Chinese, the phrase has six characters]
chapterbody 6: I laughed out loud after reading the banner!
chapterbody 7: In fact, Master Sheng Yen’s slogan “Peace of Mind Is Peace” is not wrong. It is right.
chapterbody 8: However, he forgot that one’s mind is restless as long as one is a human being. Everyone has afflictions. How can one achieve peace of mind?
chapterbody 10: Let me provide two jokes as examples.
chapterbody 12: The first joke is:
chapterbody 13: Old Mr. Wang congratulated young Mr. Li, “Congratulations to both of you. True love always results in marriage. Your love finally produced results.”
chapterbody 14: Mr. Li said with a wry smile, “Yikes! We have been married for only three months and my wife is already pregnant. It seems lovers eventually become a tired couple.”
chapterbody 16: The second joke is:
chapterbody 17: A patient told the doctor, “I’m very upset. I obviously have amnesia. What can I do?”
chapterbody 18: The doctor said, “Go home! You just need to forget about it. Then, your illness will be healed!”
chapterbody 20: Hahaha!
chapterbody 21: I knowingly smile at these two jokes. Although jokes, they contain hidden truths. This proves that afflictions are everywhere.
chapterbody 23: Having “peace of mind” is not easy for people!
chapterbody 24: I have a key point here which I have said before and repeat it often:
chapterbody 25: First, everyone will die.
chapterbody 26: Second, everything will fade away.
chapterbody 27: Third, all matter will decay.
chapterbody 28: I often think of these three sentences. By thinking of these three sentences, one will have peace of mind.
chapterbody 29: Therefore, these three sentences are the key points for attaining peace of mind.
chapterbody 31: In my life, I have encountered numerous people, events, and things. One wave after another arises endlessly...
chapterbody 32: Thinking of the key points of these three sentences keeps my mind peaceful!
chapterbody 34: Thus:
chapterbody 35: Peace of mind - Peace.
chapterbody 36: Peace of mind is peace.
chapterbody 37: This is Master Sheng Yen’s slogan.
chapterbody 38: However, I only use the key points. People will die, everything will fade away, and all matter will decay. As a result, the mind will naturally become peaceful.
chapterbody 40: Having people shout slogans is not enough; you have to teach sentient beings how to make their minds peaceful!
chapterbody 41: Perhaps you do not know the source of these three sentences. The three sentences are drawn from the truths realized by Shakyamuni Buddha, which are:
chapterbody 42: Impermanence,
chapterbody 43: No self,
chapterbody 44: Nirvana, and
chapterbody 45: Quiescence.
chapterbody 47: Someone said, “What Grand Master Lu spoke is Nihilism!”
chapterbody 48: I replied, “No, it is not!”
chapterbody 50: I keep my body and mind unhindered (peace of mind) and free from attachment. With this attitude, I do my best to handle people and matters. I make great effort to spread the Buddhadharma and to cultivate. This is bodhi and compassion. However, I do not care about the outcome. This is the ultimate wisdom of Buddha’s teaching.
chapterbody 51: I understand this, so I have no afflictions and nothing matters!
chapterbody 57: Although you already know nothing is to be attained, can you still “transcend” the temptations?
chapterbody 58: Sheng-yen Lu

chapterbody: ’ll tell you three jokes first:
chapterbody 3: A wife looking in a mirror angrily stated, “It seems I am getting older and older, and getting uglier and uglier.”
chapterbody 4: Her husband responded coldly, “You seem to have a knack for the obvious.”
chapterbody 5: (Changes due to the passage of time.)
chapterbody 7: Also:
chapterbody 8: A said to B, “My wife often says that marriage can change a man, make him grow, and learn a lot of things.”
chapterbody 9: B agreed, “She is right. After marriage, I learned to do laundry, grocery shopping, cooking, and housework.”
chapterbody 10: (Changes after being married.)
chapterbody 12: Also:
chapterbody 13: A wife asked her husband, “Before marriage, when you and I met, you always said that your heart was beating like a ‘blindly-running deer.’ Do you still have this feeling?”
chapterbody 14: The husband replied, “Not anymore!”
chapterbody 15: “Why?” His wife asked.
chapterbody 16: The husband answered, “The deer already crashed into something and died a long time ago!”
chapterbody 18: Hahaha!
chapterbody 19: I use these three jokes as examples to emphasize “changes.” However, after attaining enlightenment, will a practitioner truly gain strength to alter their habits and afflictions?
chapterbody 21: We often say, “Mountains can be moved, but one’s nature is hard to change.”
chapterbody 22: “People’s habits are deeply rooted, and to change a person’s stubborn nature is not easy.”
chapterbody 23: I often say that water can extinguish a fire. However, when a fire is large and the water supply is low, it is like trying to put out a burning pile of wood with a cup of water. How can one do it?
chapterbody 24: Although fire can boil water, if there is only a little flame, how long will it take to boil a full bathtub of water?
chapterbody 26: By the same token, when one has just attained enlightenment, how can one free oneself from the entanglement of “money, power, fame, status, love and enjoyment” while one still has karmic hindrances, habits, affinities, the seven human emotions, and six desires accumulated over lifetimes.
chapterbody 28: That is why there is “cultivation after enlightenment.”
chapterbody 30: However, whether you can succeed or not all depends on whether you have enough power of concentration and wisdom. (Many enlightened practitioners still have greed, anger, ignorance, doubts and arrogance.)
chapterbody 32: Everyone knows:
chapterbody 33: Everyone will die.
chapterbody 34: Everything will fade away.
chapterbody 35: All objects will decay.
chapterbody 36: However, when the temptations arrive, are you able to “instantaneously cease” them? Although you already know nothing is to be attained, can you still “transcend” the temptations?
chapterbody 38: This is why “cultivation starts after enlightenment!”
chapterbody 40: You have to achieve the following:
chapterbody 41: To reach the other shore.
chapterbody 42: To liberate oneself from the entanglement of afflictions and habits.
chapterbody 43: To end secular life, transcend the secular world, and be enlightened.
chapterbody 45: This indeed is not easy, so the key point I use is “perseverance.”
chapterbody 47: “Perseverance” is necessary in one’s whole life. Myriad practices subsumed in the six perfections are inseparable from “perseverance.”
chapterbody 49: I have many disciples who have “awakened their minds,” but still a lot of them show worldly attachments. It is very difficult to transcend the six paths of reincarnation. I advise all holy disciples to exercise “perseverance,” “Instantaneous cessation” and “Instantaneously transcend” all temptations. Only then can one’s Buddha-nature appear naturally.
chapterbody 50: One must be able to be “instantaneously enlightened.”
chapterbody 51: Also one must be able to “cultivate gradually.”
chapterbody 57: As long as there is no nagging, the pure Buddhanature will show up naturally!
chapterbody 58: Sheng-yen Lu

chapterbody: omeone asked me, “How do you witness the Buddha-nature?”
chapterbody 2: I replied, “Naturally.”
chapterbody 4: I was asked again, “Why is it natural? Doesn’t one discover Buddhanature through perseverance?”
chapterbody 5: I replied, “No! As long as one removes deluded thoughts, the Buddha-nature is right there.”
chapterbody 7: I emphasize:
chapterbody 8: Buddha-nature always exists.
chapterbody 9: Buddha-nature is immaculate and bright.
chapterbody 10: Buddha-nature does not depend on cultivation.
chapterbody 11: One does not cultivate Buddha-nature. The Buddha-nature will appear naturally as long as one cultivates with perseverance and achieves no mind.
chapterbody 13: To state the point more clearly:

chapterbody 14: Many people are curious about the illusory body in Vajrayana. What actually is the illusory body?
chapterbody 16: Many people have asked me, “What is the illusory body?”
chapterbody 18: “The illusory body is unreal.” I replied.
chapterbody 19: However, everyone will understand the illusory body clearly when we use the term “soul” in ordinary people’s language.
chapterbody 21: The soul is essentially the “bardo.”
chapterbody 22: However, the souls have different levels:
chapterbody 23: Ghosts. (Lesser power)
chapterbody 24: Gods. (Greater power)
chapterbody 25: Nature spirits. (Ability to change)
chapterbody 26: Buddhas. (Perfection)
chapterbody 27: And many more.
chapterbody 29: In Vajrayana practice, a practitioner must cultivate until the illusory body appears before one dies.
chapterbody 31: Sometimes there can be more than one illusory body. When one has many illusory bodies, it is known as the “illusory bodies emanated beyond the flesh.”
chapterbody 33: I personally believe that the best method to cultivate the illusory body is to practice the “Mirror Gazing Yoga.”
chapterbody 34: I have discussed the “Mirror Gazing Yoga” in detail before, and will not repeat it again. The key points are:
chapterbody 35: The practitioner,
chapterbody 36: The personal deity, and
chapterbody 37: The reflection of the practitioner in the mirror
chapterbody 38: (All three merge into one)
chapterbody 40: The illusory body is born in the illusion within the illusion. This illusory body is the finest body within a practitioner’s own body. The illusory body is the emanation of one’s mind power, which is also known as the “mind-made body,” or the “spiritual body.”
chapterbody 42: I will share a secret. To achieve the fruition of Buddhahood, two elements must combine:
chapterbody 43: First, the illusory body.
chapterbody 44: Second, the pure light.
chapterbody 45: The fruition of Buddhahood is equal, deep, permanent, unique, wonderful, pure, bright, and with perfect enjoyment.
chapterbody 46: This is also the supreme Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 48: I would like everyone to pay attention to the Six Yogas of Naropa. One can refer to the Illusory Body Yoga and Clear Light Yoga in the six yogas. However, I believe that the best way to achieve the illusory body is through practicing the Mirror Gazing Yoga.
chapterbody 50: My illusory bodies are the emanations beyond the flesh. The trillions of emanation bodies manifested by the Buddha as cited in Buddhist sutras are equal to trillions of illusory bodies. I remember after my divine eye was opened by the Golden Mother of the Jade Pond, my illusory body appeared. My illusory body viewed my own past lives, and travelled to the twenty-eight heavens and through the Six Paths of Existence. This is the journey of my illusory body.
chapterbody 51: If one wants to see one’s own illusory body, one must successfully cultivate the Mirror Gazing Yoga. Only then, can one see the illusory body in meditation.

chaptername: 017 The Fundamental Template of Vajrayana Cultivation
chapterbody: typical Vajrayana sadhana contains the following steps:
chapterbody 3: 1. Respectfully pay homage.
chapterbody 4: 2. Make offerings.
chapterbody 5: 3. Chant the Fourfold Refuge Mantra.
chapterbody 6: 4. Make repentance for one’s sins.
chapterbody 7: 5. Inspire to follow the Four Immeasurable Vows (Immeasurable Loving-kindness, Immeasurable Compassion, Immeasurable Joy, and Immeasurable Equanimity).
chapterbody 8: 6. Beseech all buddhas to turn the dharma wheel.
chapterbody 9: 7. Request the buddhas to remain in the saha world and not enter into nirvana.
chapterbody 10: 8. Visualize emptiness.
chapterbody 11: 9. Visualize the principal deity.
chapterbody 12: 10. Recite the principal deity’s mantra.
chapterbody 13: 11. Enter samadhi.
chapterbody 14: 12. Recite verses of praise.
chapterbody 15: 13. Ddedication.
chapterbody 16: 14. Chant the Hundred Syllable Mantra (mending all faults).
chapterbody 18: Steps one to seven of the typical sadhana are known as the “preliminary practices;” steps eight to eleven are called “the main practices;” steps twelve to fourteen are known as “the concluding practices.” This is a complete cultivation sadhana.
chapterbody 19: In general, lamas use this set of sadhanas for their daily cultivation.
chapterbody 20: In addition, it can be utilized for a blessing or bardo deliverance ceremony. (Recitation of sutras may be added.)
chapterbody 21: The sadhana can also be used for consecration and circumambulation of pagodas and temples. Many yogis secluded in mountain caves also practice with such rituals every day.
chapterbody 23: As for the higher level practices of cultivating qi, channels and light drops, or specializing in Inner Fire Yoga, the same sadhana can be used for petitioning:
chapterbody 24: Blessings from one’s guru;
chapterbody 25: Blessings from one’s principal deity;
chapterbody 26: Support from one’s dharma protectors.
chapterbody 27: Of course, rituals are created by humans, so naturally one can recite additional sutras or mantras that one likes. If you are travelling by boat, aircraft or vehicle, of course, it is okay to only carry out “the main practices.” All of these depend on the conditions and circumstances.
chapterbody 29: The four tantras requires one to learn the formal sadhana practices, such as setting up the altar (mandala), the skills to produce “offerings.”
chapterbody 30: The Action Tantra sadhana,
chapterbody 31: The Performance Tantra sadhana,
chapterbody 32: The Yoga sadhana, and
chapterbody 33: The Highest Yoga Tantra sadhana.
chapterbody 34: In addition, the various principal deities all have their own different practices. Although the essentials of the practices are similar, there are some different features. One must pay attention to the differences, in order to differentiate each practice.
chapterbody 36: The practice of Guhyasamaja, Cakrasamvara, Hevajra, Yamantaka, Mahottara Heruka, Kalachakra, and others are not all identical.
chapterbody 37: There are similarities and differences. One must learn the subtle and profound characteristics of each practice.
chapterbody 39: No matter what sadhanas, the practitioner must have faith and a premise, which is inseparable from the “Three Principal Aspects of the Path” of Je Tsongkhapa:
chapterbody 40: 1. Supra-mundane-mind;
chapterbody 41: 2. Bodhicitta;
chapterbody 42: 3. The Middle Way.
chapterbody 44: Although I have written so much today, each step of the sadhanas in fact has its own key. These numerous keys are all accumulated from my past experience. Without the teaching by a qualified master, I believe that practicing Vajrayana Buddhism will not be easy.

chaptername: 018 Benefits of Performing the Great Homage
chapterbody: disciple asked me, “What are the benefits for the Tibetans who pay homage every three steps starting from their hometowns to the Jokhang and Ramoche Temples in Lhasa?”
chapterbody 3: I replied: The homage that the Tibetans perform every three steps is a full prostration, which is different from kneel and bow in the middle kingdom.
chapterbody 4: The former throws oneself flat on the ground face downward. (Full-body worship) The latter kneels and touches the ground with the forehead. (Half-body worship)
chapterbody 5: The great homage of Tibetan Vajrayana Buddhism belongs to the former.
chapterbody 6: Most Tibetans perform the great homage while circumambulating and worshipping temples, pagodas, holy mountains, and holy lakes.
chapterbody 8: The benefits are:
chapterbody 9: To strengthen one’s determination and perseverance for cultivation.
chapterbody 10: To accumulate provisions for cultivation.
chapterbody 11: To appreciate the value of being a human being.
chapterbody 12: To exercise one’s physical body.
chapterbody 13: To sever evil deeds and carry out good deeds.
chapterbody 14: To train one’s ability to concentrate.
chapterbody 15: To subdue one’s own arrogance.
chapterbody 16: To form an affinity with buddhas.
chapterbody 17: And many more.
chapterbody 19: The disciple asked: “Wow, I have never thought of so many benefits! Grand Master Lu, which do you think are the most important?”
chapterbody 21: I thought for a moment and answered:
chapterbody 22: To train one’s ability to concentrate and subdue one’s own arrogance are the most important.
chapterbody 23: The reason is that when performing great homage, one concentrates on the head, hands, feet, and the whole body. Since every part of one’s body works simultaneously, it is not easy for one to generate improper thoughts. Without improper thoughts one focuses wholeheartedly.
chapterbody 25: In addition, full prostration places a practitioner in a humble position. With the sky above one’s head and the ground below one’s face, the posture shows the most respect and one’s arrogance will disappear.
chapterbody 27: The main purpose for practitioners in cultivating their minds is to eliminate their thoughts of greed, hate, stupidity, doubt, and arrogance.
chapterbody 28: If arrogant thoughts are not eliminated, pride will arise. We know that pride will lead one to the Path of Asuras, and the desire for competition and fighting. Fighting and competition can unconsciously turn one to evil.
chapterbody 30: The disciple asked: “Are there key points in paying great homage?”
chapterbody 31: I replied: The following is my personal experience.
chapterbody 32: While paying great homage, I chant: “Homage to 36 Trillion, 119 Thousand and 500 Buddhas by the same name and identification of Amitabha.”
chapterbody 34: In my mind, I visualize that trillions of emanation bodies of Amitabha Buddhas appear in the space before my eyes.
chapterbody 35: Performing a single prostration in such a manner is equal to chanting countless buddha epithets and simultaneously worshipping countless Amitabha Buddhas.
chapterbody 36: As a result, one will accumulate endless provisions and merits.
chapterbody 38: I would like to tell a joke:
chapterbody 39: A woman said to her husband, “We have been married for years. However, you have never say anything nice about me but only mention my shortcomings. You should mention some of my good attributes!”
chapterbody 40: Her husband replied: “Except for all the shortcomings, the rest is all good!”
chapterbody 41: The wife said: “Then say it! What good attributes do I have?”
chapterbody 42: The husband replied: “Oh, there’s nothing to say!”
chapterbody 44: I mean to say:

chaptername: 019 The Secret of Changing from One Offering to Many
chapterbody: n Vajrayana practice, there are several kinds of achievements.
chapterbody 2: In summary, there are three major achievements:
chapterbody 3: First, mantra chanting accomplishment.
chapterbody 4: Second, homa accomplishment.
chapterbody 5: Third, samadhi accomplishment.
chapterbody 7: The first achievement is to chant mantras until the appearance of a great pure light.
chapterbody 8: The second achievement is to practice fire offerings until perfect completion of blessedness and virtue is achieved.
chapterbody 9: The third achievement is the great achievement of awakening the mind and seeing the Buddha-nature in samadhi.
chapterbody 11: Some people say: “In cultivation of Vajrayana Buddhism, it is all about making offerings, because the offering dharma is of primary importance.”
chapterbody 13: There is a Great Mandala Offering in Vajrayana Buddhism. I think this offering practice condenses the universe into a mandala with magnificent offerings placed on the mandala.
chapterbody 14: The mandala has multiple layers:
chapterbody 15: The dharma wheel is erected at the top – a symbol of the highest light achievement of the universe.
chapterbody 16: Offerings are placed in the middle – these include gold, silver, jewelry, medicinal herbs, and rice.
chapterbody 17: At the bottom of the mandala – are stones and sand.
chapterbody 18: The Tibetan Vajrayana Buddhism sadhana for The Great Mandala Offering is complicated. (One should consult a vajra master for the details.)
chapterbody 19: There are also thirty-seven offerings,
chapterbody 20: Eight offerings in front of the holy shrine,
chapterbody 21: Five mounds of offerings,
chapterbody 22: Rice offering,
chapterbody 23: Water offering,
chapterbody 24: Fire offering,
chapterbody 25: And many more.
chapterbody 27: A disciple asked me: “What is the purpose of offering?”
chapterbody 28: I replied: “It is to accumulate provisions for blessings, virtue and wisdom.”
chapterbody 30: The disciple asked: “What’s the key point?”
chapterbody 31: I replied: “The key point is effort. One who provides strenuous efforts makes the best offering. Therefore, the merit of offering does not originate from the seven precious objects and eight treasures, but from the heart of oneself. Tremendous motivation leads to tremendous merit.”
chapterbody 33: I have told my disciples that my experience is the following:
chapterbody 34: The Great Mandala Offering that I practice is “the body, speech, and mind are offered to the buddhas, bodhisattvas, vajra protectors, dakas and dakinis, all the devas, and beings in the Six Paths…….”
chapterbody 36: Pure offering of body.
chapterbody 37: Mantra offering through speech.
chapterbody 38: Visualized offering within the mind.
chapterbody 39: As for offering food, one should offer all the foods before eating them. One should transform the foods into an expanse of offerings through one’s mind power.
chapterbody 41: As for offering clothing, one should transform one’s clothes into an expanse of offerings using one’s mind power.
chapterbody 42: As for offering one’s home, one should transform one’s house, items, bedding, and everything into an expanse of offerings.
chapterbody 43: As for offering one’s transportation, one should transform one’s car and all means of transportation into an expanse of offerings.
chapterbody 44: Whenever one experiences pleasure, one should transform the happiness into an expanse of offerings.
chapterbody 45: One should transform jewelry, all the seven precious objects and eight treasures, into an expanse of offerings.
chapterbody 46: And so on and so forth… etc.
chapterbody 48: Honestly, I would like to tell all the disciples that one should transform everything, all one’s behaviors, practices, and daily life activities, into an expanse of offerings, by transforming each and every one from one to many.
chapterbody 49: (This is the key)
chapterbody 51: I say:
chapterbody 52: In the future, if I reincarnate as a man, I will still be number one in the world:
chapterbody 53: I’ll transform a little food into endless delicious foods.
chapterbody 54: I’ll transform a beautiful woman into celestial maidens of the ten directions and three times.
chapterbody 56: I’ll transform a piece of gold into a gold mountain.
chapterbody 57: I’ll transform a piece of land into the whole planet earth.
chapterbody 58: I’ll transform my single mind into numerous transformations. (Expansive bodhicitta aspirations)
chapterbody 60: Persons of high scope take refuge to save sentient beings from the sea of suffering and effect universal deliverance.

chapterbody 26: I believe:
chapterbody 27: "Only by practicing Buddhism can we be liberated from all the afflictions in life. The Buddhadharma is the key to opening the door of wisdom. In order to end the cycle of reincarnation, to achieve nirvana, and to become an enlightened person, one must cultivate.”
chapterbody 29: At the beginning of cultivation, one must first take refuge with a guru, the buddha, dharma, and sangha.
chapterbody 30: Persons of modest scope take refuge because they fear the pains associated with the animal realm, hungry ghost realm, and hell realm. They seek the comforts in the human world and the heavens.
chapterbody 31: Persons of medium scope take refuge aiming to stop the cycle of reincarnation and enter nirvana.
chapterbody 32: Persons of high scope take refuge to save sentient beings from the sea of suffering and effect universal deliverance.
chapterbody 34: In terms of growing faith in taking refuge:
chapterbody 35: Some people read my books and listened to my dharma discourses.
chapterbody 36: Some experienced spiritual responses, and their pains released by my blessings.
chapterbody 37: Some witnessed others who received spiritual responses.
chapterbody 39: I personally feel that many people have the initial resolve to seek enlightenment, but they don’t have the spiritual perseverance, which is a common problem amongst human beings. If people do not have strong faith and a firm belief, most will give up halfway. People’s minds often change, because the mind is impermanent. I often say people’s minds are like water, flowing from one place to another. They take refuge with one master at one moment and with another at the next moment. They have faith in Buddhism at one moment, and then don’t in the next moment. This is after all normal behavior, and not unexpected.
chapterbody 41: Therefore, there are rules in Vajrayana teachings:
chapterbody 42: One must recite over a hundred thousand times the Fourfold Refuge Mantra:
chapterbody 43: Namo Guru bei. (Take refuge in the guru)
chapterbody 44: Namo Buddha ye. (Take refuge in the buddha)
chapterbody 45: Namo Dharma ye. (Take refuge in the dharma)
chapterbody 46: Namo Sangha ye. (Take refuge in the sangha)
chapterbody 47: My taking refuge was triggered by the Golden Mother of the Jade Pond opening my divine eye. I saw the White Maha Padmakumara as my former life and his pure land, the Maha Twin Lotus Ponds. From that moment, I started to wholeheartedly study Buddhism, study the Buddhadharma, and behave virtuously.
chapterbody 49: The reasons that my faith has never wavered are:
chapterbody 50: 1. I have truly “seen.”
chapterbody 51: 2. I have spiritual responses.
chapterbody 52: 3. I have dharma power.
chapterbody 53: 5. I have awakened the mind and seen the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 54: When in the future, my holy disciples reach the same attainments by firmly believing that Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-yen Lu has indeed achieved the six abilities mentioned above and continuously practice the True Buddha Tantric Dharma, they will naturally never lose the faith. These are holy disciples truly with long lasting bodhicitta.
chapterbody 56: 4. I am able to save sentient beings.
chapterbody 57: 6. I have attained Buddhahood.
chapterbody 58: In order to save sentient beings, it is necessary for me to let disciples have spiritual responses, so that they will have strong faith.
chapterbody 59: Wise disciples should be able to see my tireless efforts and achievements.
chapterbody 65: The key point for disciples never giving up occurs when they have spiritual responses which induce faith.
chapterbody 66: If not, then continue to be like water flowing aimlessly, or get lost.

chapterbody: often teach people to recite the Vajrasattva Mantra:
chapterbody 3: The short form is: Om, be-dza, sah-do, ah, hum, pei.
chapterbody 4: The long form is the Hundred Syllable Mantra.
chapterbody 5: We know that the Primordial Buddha is the highest, the Five Dhyani Buddhas are below him, and finally there is Vajrasattva.
chapterbody 6: Therefore, Vajrasattva is the dharma prince of the Five Dhyani Buddhas.
chapterbody 7: Vajrayana Buddhism considers Vajrasattva as the founder.
chapterbody 9: The Vajrasattva Mantra has two major significances:
chapterbody 10: 1. Emptiness.
chapterbody 11: 2. Brightness and emptiness.
chapterbody 12: Chanting the Hundred Syllable Mantra is the great dharma of repentance.
chapterbody 14: Why should one repent?
chapterbody 15: One should repent because sentient beings give rise to thoughts that manifest nothing but negative karma.
chapterbody 16: In addition, each person, in the present or countless past lives, has accumulated a great deal of negative karma. If one does not repent, the negative karma will become obstacles, which will prevent one from having achievements in their spiritual practice. Consequently, one will fall into the three evil paths.
chapterbody 17: This is the main reason for us to practice the dharma of repentance.
chapterbody 19: First, one should visualize that there is a white lotus flower with a thousand petals above one’s crown chakra. On the white lotus is Vajrasattva in a father-mother form.
chapterbody 20: In the heart chakra of Vajrasattva, there is a Hum () syllable surrounded by the Hundred Syllable Mantra.
chapterbody 21: While reciting the Hundred Syllable Mantra, the practitioner visualizes the syllables transforming into a white nectar. The white nectar continuously flows into the practitioner’s body through the crown chakra. As a result, all diseases within the body transform into black blood, all harmful obstructions transform into poisonous insects, and all negative karma transforms into black smoke.
chapterbody 22: The black blood, poisonous insects and black smoke are discharged from the skin pores, the anus and the soles of the feet.
chapterbody 23: The practitioner is purified and perfect. One then recites the Hundred Syllable Mantra for 7 times, 21 times, or 108 times. The more the better.
chapterbody 24: Finally, one recites the Repentance Verse.
chapterbody 26: I personally feel that the key for practicing repentance is:
chapterbody 27: “To repent sincerely.”
chapterbody 28: Repentance is not a formality, nor a ritual, but a practice one must perform wholeheartedly.
chapterbody 29: A sage once said, “All sins originate from the heart, so one should repent from the heart.”
chapterbody 31: Vajrasattva made the following vow:
chapterbody 32: “If sentient beings commit the five cardinal sins, and have broken the Samaya Pledge, as long as they hear my epithet, have faith in me, and recite the king of mantras, which is the Hundred Syllable Mantra, then all hindrances will be cleared.”
chapterbody 34: I believe that the Vajrasattva practice is the number one repentance dharma.
chapterbody 36: Finally, I would like to tell everyone these important key points:
chapterbody 37: If one’s karmic hindrances have been eliminated by practicing repentance, auspicious signs must manifest. The auspicious signs can manifest as the following:
chapterbody 38: Dreams of eating a white substance and spitting out a black substance.
chapterbody 39: Dreams of expelling black blood and filth through the anus.
chapterbody 40: Dreams of poisonous insects crawling out from the skin pores.
chapterbody 41: Dreams of black smoke emitting from the body, or from the soles of the feet.
chapterbody 42: One sees that their body is transparent, radiating pure light.
chapterbody 43: One is standing on a lotus or riding upon a heavenly horse.
chapterbody 44: One is flying in the empty sky.
chapterbody 45: Dreams of Vajrasattva or one’s personal deity placing their palm upon one’s head.
chapterbody 46: Receiving a prediction from one’s personal deity...
chapterbody 47: These are the auspicious signs from practicing the repentance dharma.
chapterbody 49: I would like to tell a joke:
chapterbody 50: A family was about to leave on a trip.
chapterbody 51: The wife was slowly choosing outfits to wear.
chapterbody 52: The husband was impatiently shouting, “Hurry up, stop putting off anymore!”
chapterbody 55: Equanimity is the basis of developing bodhicitta, which is a heart with equality and without discrimination.
chapterbody 56: Their son interrupted, “Dad, Mother is putting on clothes, not putting off.”
chapterbody 57: Hahaha! Repentance should not be delayed!

chapterbody 6: I often tell this story:
chapterbody 7: A profession robber attempted to steal from a village.
chapterbody 8: Upon entering the village, he noticed a baby who had just learned how to crawl was dangerously close to the edge of a well. The baby was only a step from falling into the well.
chapterbody 9: Instinctively, he stepped forward, picked up and placed the infant in a safe area.
chapterbody 10: Afterwards, the robber continued to the village to carry out the robbery.
chapterbody 12: My comment is that:
chapterbody 13: Even a robber has compassion.
chapterbody 15: As spiritual practitioners, we of course should develop the bodhicitta which includes the so-called Aspiration Bodhicitta, Action Bodhicitta, and Absolute Bodhicitta.
chapterbody 17: We often say:
chapterbody 18: To give people happiness – benevolence.
chapterbody 19: To remove people’s sufferings - compassion.
chapterbody 20: To fulfill one’s vows happily - joy.
chapterbody 21: To give equally without discrimination - equanimity.
chapterbody 22: I understand that the focus of a bodhisattva is on equanimity, which is the key point.
chapterbody 23: “Benevolence without specificity and great compassion based on sameness in essence” is not a hackneyed phrase, but means equanimity.
chapterbody 25: We are aware:
chapterbody 26: The human body leads to suffering.
chapterbody 27: The human world is a burning house.
chapterbody 28: Human beings are all so pitiful, especially those who are your enemies and those who hate you. They do not understand, have muddled thoughts, and are not awakened. Those who have resentment and hatred are even more pitiful.
chapterbody 29: You have to save them.
chapterbody 31: This is equanimity.
chapterbody 32: Equanimity is the basis of developing bodhicitta, which is a heart with equality and without discrimination.
chapterbody 34: Think of others without considering oneself.
chapterbody 35: This is called being a bodhisattva.
chapterbody 36: The key point of Aspiration Bodhicitta is - to “exchange oneself with others.”
chapterbody 37: The key point of Action Bodhicitta is - the “wisdom of equality.”
chapterbody 38: The key point of Absolute Bodhicitta is - “emptiness.”
chapterbody 40: There are three ways to develop bodhicitta:
chapterbody 41: King-like bodhicitta – one achieves Buddhahood first, then helps others to achieve it.
chapterbody 42: Boatman-like bodhicitta – one reaches Buddhahood with other sentient beings.
chapterbody 43: Shepherd-like bodhicitta – one helps others to achieve Buddhahood before one achieves it.
chapterbody 45: In this life I, Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-yen Lu, descended to the saha world to save sentient beings. The main purpose is to play a role in a drama of saving sentient beings that includes:
chapterbody 46: Donating wealth - to the Sheng-Yen Lu Foundation.
chapterbody 47: Teaching Buddhadharma – through numerous dharma disseminations and empowerments.
chapterbody 48: Bestowing fearlessness – to help others cross to the other shore together.
chapterbody 49: Five precepts - to prevent misconduct.
chapterbody 50: Ten Wholesome Actions – to benefit sentient beings.
chapterbody 51: Patience – others and one-self are equal.
chapterbody 52: Perseverance – laziness and lethargy must be abandoned.
chapterbody 53: Wisdom – listening, contemplating and cultivating Buddhadharma to cross to the other shore.
chapterbody 54: This is a drama of saving sentient beings. When the drama is over, it will be time for me to leave!
chapterbody 56: Meditative – being detached to everything.
chapterbody 60: The Buddhadharma benefits both human and celestial realms, therefore producing the greatest merits and field of blessings.

chaptername: 023 What Merits the Greatest Field of Blessings?
chapterbody: recall a joke that goes like this:
chapterbody 3: A student said to a math teacher, “My father would like to listen to your lecture. Is it okay?”
chapterbody 4: The math teacher replied, “Certainly, is he interested in math?”
chapterbody 5: The student answered, “No! My father thinks that sleeping is the greatest blessing!”
chapterbody 6: The math teacher asked, “What does sleeping have to do with mathematics?”
chapterbody 7: The student answered, “My father is a chronic insomniac. All treatments have been useless. After hearing how I can easily fall asleep in your math class, he wants to come and try!”
chapterbody 8: Hahaha!
chapterbody 9: The greatest field of blessings is sleep!
chapterbody 11: What bears the greatest field of blessings for practitioners?
chapterbody 12: I believe it is:
chapterbody 13: To pray for buddhas to remain in the saha world.
chapterbody 14: To pray for buddhas to turn the dharma wheel.
chapterbody 15: This yields the greatest field of blessings.
chapterbody 16: Some people think that giving produces the greatest field of blessings. This is also correct, as one can receive the karmic merits of the Heavens through giving.
chapterbody 17: Some people think that providing offerings to the Sangha will produce the greatest field of blessings. This is also correct, as providing offerings to the Sangha, one will gather a great field of blessings, and easily receive the blessings of buddhas and bodhisattvas.
chapterbody 18: Some people think that taking care of sick people will produce the greatest field of blessings. This is also correct, as sickness is the most painful thing. If one can remove the sufferings of patients, the virtues and merits are naturally great.
chapterbody 20: However, for one to truly earn the greatest field of blessings is:
chapterbody 21: To pray for buddhas to remain in the saha world.
chapterbody 22: To pray for buddhas to turn the dharma wheel.
chapterbody 23: This occurs because only by so doing, the Buddhadharma will exist in the saha world.
chapterbody 24: Buddhadharma benefits both human and celestial realms, therefore producing the greatest merits and field of blessings.
chapterbody 25: If there is no Buddhadharma, what is the field of blessings?
chapterbody 27: I remember that:
chapterbody 28: When Shakyamuni Buddha was about to enter nirvana, there was a layman named Cunda who prayed for the Buddha to remain in the saha world.
chapterbody 29: The Buddha delayed his entering into nirvana for three months and discoursed one more significant sutra.
chapterbody 31: Moreover:
chapterbody 32: Immediately after achieving enlightenment under the bodhi tree for forty-nine days, Shakyamuni Buddha only wanted to liberate himself into nirvana without teaching the Buddhadharma or saving sentient beings.
chapterbody 33: Then, Maha Brahma and Indra descended.
chapterbody 34: They offered the Buddha a right spiral conch and a golden wheel.
chapterbody 35: They beseeched the Buddha to expound the dharma.
chapterbody 36: The Buddha then spoke the dharma and turned the dharma wheel.
chapterbody 37: In this way, Buddhism was able to spread throughout the world.
chapterbody 38: This really merits the greatest field of blessings!
chapterbody 40: Which one is greater, a worldly field of blessings or a supramundane field of blessings?
chapterbody 41: My key on the field of blessings is:
chapterbody 42: To pray for buddhas to remain in the saha world.
chapterbody 43: To pray for buddhas to turn the dharma wheel.
chapterbody 44: This is the greatest key for the perfect accomplishment of provisions.
chapterbody 46: I composed two verses:
chapterbody 47: Field of blessings, field of blessings
chapterbody 48: Chased by everyone diligently
chapterbody 49: Accumulation of gold, silver and treasures
chapterbody 50: None can be carried away from the world
chapterbody 52: Additionally...
chapterbody 53: Is not as good as having karmic affinity with a buddha
chapterbody 54: This is the true key for the greatest field of blessings!
chapterbody 56: Field of blessings, field of blessings
chapterbody 57: Pray for buddhas to turn the dharma wheel Merits will manifest before one’s eyes
chapterbody 59: In a condition free of any state whatsoever, there is no need to explain anything.

chapterbody: would like to tell a joke first that is related to the topic:
chapterbody 3: Mr. Wang opened a coffin store.
chapterbody 4: A customer entered and asked, “This coffin is half the price of the other coffin, but I looked carefully and found no difference between the two!”
chapterbody 6: Mr. Wang replied, “The two coffins look almost the same, but the expensive one was designed according to the shape of a human body that allows people to rest more comfortably. Do you want to try?”
chapterbody 8: The customer was stunned and speechless.
chapterbody 9: Hahaha!
chapterbody 10: What I mean to say is, once people pass away and return to the state of emptiness, what is comfortable? What is uncomfortable?
chapterbody 12: I say:
chapterbody 13: After one passes away, everything stops and the body rests in quiescence.
chapterbody 15: In Vajrayana Buddhism, one often learns to “observe emptiness,” “meditate” and “enter into the state of samadhi.”
chapterbody 16: This is the nature of the absolute emptiness.
chapterbody 17: The key of body – is being immovable.
chapterbody 18: The key of speech – is being silent.
chapterbody 19: The key of mind - is being tranquil.
chapterbody 20: Accomplishing these three points, one reaches the nature of the absolute emptiness.
chapterbody 21: These three points look easy, but are quite difficult to achieve.
chapterbody 23: In regards to the body:
chapterbody 24: One avoids all earthly things, does not even attempt Buddhist activities, and meditates motionlessly in the full lotus position.
chapterbody 25: In regards to speech:
chapterbody 26: One remains silent which includes not reciting mantras audibly or silently.
chapterbody 27: In regards to the mind:
chapterbody 28: One ceases all delusive thoughts, does not think about the past, present, nor the future, and is not confused. Everything ceases suddenly.
chapterbody 30: One does not visualize the personal deity nor the shrine.
chapterbody 31: One does not observe the mind, nor is attached to any thoughts.
chapterbody 32: (It seems that this is the most difficult part, since people generally have a lot of wandering thoughts. Even thoughts are not allowed.)
chapterbody 34: Contemplation of ending thoughts is not even allowed.
chapterbody 35: This is the nature of the absolute emptiness.
chapterbody 37: This is:
chapterbody 38: “No beginning or end.”
chapterbody 39: “No birth or death.”
chapterbody 40: “Naturally so, naturally so.”
chapterbody 41: “The absolute emptiness and the absolute emptiness.”
chapterbody 42: (This is light and the illusory body uniting together.)
chapterbody 44: This is also:
chapterbody 45: The light and Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 46: Not forgetting and not attaching.
chapterbody 47: Existing at all times and without discrimination.
chapterbody 48: Marvelous existence and excellent emptiness.
chapterbody 49: In a condition free of any state whatsoever, there is no need to explain anything. Since it is ineffable and indefinable, any explanation will be meaningless.
chapterbody 51: In this condition, all sentient beings can be subdued. This turns into quiescence, eliminates confusion, increases virtues, enables fearlessness, creates blessings, enlightens, liberates sentient beings, nurtures compassion, and awakens supernatural powers ……
chapterbody 53: This is “non-grasping and non-attachment.”
chapterbody 54: “The body, mind, dharma, and nature are all empty.”
chapterbody 56: This key is “naturally so.”
chapterbody 57: This is also what Manjushri Bodhisattva referred to as:
chapterbody 59: If one practices these three dharmas properly and precisely, it is very easy for one to enter the analytical meditation of the completion stage.

chaptername: 025 The Leading Practices of the Vajrayana Completion Stage
chapterbody: have explained much about the Vajrayana completion stage during my dharma discourses. Most of the essential key points have been described in detail. For example:
chapterbody 2: Channels, qi, light drops,
chapterbody 3: The Nine Cycle Breathing Exercise,
chapterbody 4: Vajra Chanting,
chapterbody 5: Treasure Vase Breathing Practice,
chapterbody 6: Inner Fire,
chapterbody 7: Light drops.
chapterbody 8: I have elaborated on the above practices, and the key points can be found throughout my dharma talks. One must not take those key points as only knowledge, but practice them earnestly and steadfastly.
chapterbody 10: For example:
chapterbody 11: The Nine Cycle Breathing Exercise consists of three variations of visualization and breathing. Each variation requires three repetitions.
chapterbody 12: The key points are:
chapterbody 13: Concentration.
chapterbody 14: Power of Mindfulness.
chapterbody 15: Breathing gently, slowly, and long.
chapterbody 16: Why should one breathe gently - the key point is to be “meticulous.”
chapterbody 17: Why should one breathe slowly - the key point is to be “tranquil.”
chapterbody 18: Why should one breathe long - the key point is to “enter quietude.”
chapterbody 20: Moreover:
chapterbody 21: Vajra Chanting visualizes the syllables () “om,” () “ah,” and () “hum.”
chapterbody 22: Om - Represents the blessing of the buddhas of the three times (in white).
chapterbody 23: Ah - Represents the words of the buddhas of the three times (in red).
chapterbody 24: Hum - Represents the mind of the buddhas of the three times (in blue).
chapterbody 25: We know that Vajra Chanting is the Vajrayana unification cultivation of chanting, visualization, and holding the qi.
chapterbody 27: First, breathe in; second, hold your breath; third, breathe out.
chapterbody 28: I would like to inform you:
chapterbody 29: These three seed syllables have great merit, which can change one’s fate.
chapterbody 30: The key points of the three seed syllables are:
chapterbody 31: Om (Dharma Body).
chapterbody 32: Ah (Bliss Body).
chapterbody 33: Hum (Emanation Body).
chapterbody 34: One breathes in the short seed syllable “om,” holds the breath with the “ah” seed syllable for a period of time, then breathes out slowly the “hum” seed syllable.
chapterbody 36: The main purpose of the practice is to enter the stage of quietude, and then to progress to the stage of no birth.
chapterbody 38: In addition:
chapterbody 39: The Treasure Vase Breathing Practice is an internal qi practice, in which one visualizes three channels.
chapterbody 40: First, gently force downward the Ascending Qi, then raise upward the Descending Qi. The two streams of qi merge under the navel, forming a vase-like shape.
chapterbody 42: There are four stages in this practice:
chapterbody 43: Guiding – one breathes in air long and deeply down to the lower abdomen, and raises the Descending Qi, then the air and qi merge.
chapterbody 44: Filling – one holds the breath for thirty seconds, the longer the better.
chapterbody 45: Expelling - one visualizes the qi expelling out of one’s skin pores.
chapterbody 46: Projecting – one visualizes the qi projecting to the top of the head, and then releasing through the nose.
chapterbody 48: Key points:
chapterbody 49: Force the qi to flow into the middle channel. This is called transmuting the expedient qi to the wisdom qi.
chapterbody 51: Safety precautions:
chapterbody 52: One should use the nostrils to breathe in and out. Do not practice when one is sick. One should not perform the four stages more than twenty one cycles. The best time to perform the practice is in the early morning on an empty stomach.
chapterbody 53: Important note:
chapterbody 55: If one practices these three dharmas properly and precisely, it is very easy for one to enter the analytical meditation of the completion stage. The three practices are the keys, and also the leading practices of the completion stage. I have explained this very clearly in my dharma talks.
chapterbody 57: As there are differences in the practice for males and females, one must consult a vajra master for more information. Furthermore, the practice involves risks. One must not take it lightly and be cautious when practicing.
chapterbody 58: I personally think that the “Nine Cycle Breathing Exercise,” “Vajra Chanting,” and the “Treasure Vase Breathing Practice” are very important.
chapterbody 60: A lady said to her husband, “A lot of men say I have a pretty face and fit body. You never say that to me.”
chapterbody 61: Her husband replied, “Well, you know, I don’t like to tell jokes.”
chapterbody 65: The three dharmas all have complex keys. If one wishes to learn them in depth, one should purchase my DVD dharma talks.
